region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu / Chennai), adapted in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb curry entry; no specific Madras-style variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Madras-specific lamb curry code. A0CKP (lamb meat dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for Lamb Madras curry. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional curry variants at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Madras is approximately 250 g, usually accompanied by rice or bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Madras — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Madras (cr_id G00010855). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (USDA NDB 17290) proportioned to the fat content reported by HPB. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat curry with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Madras. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed meat curry with vegetables, which is protein- and fat-dominant with low carbohydrate content (6.3 g/100g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (35 × 6.29 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308 mg/100g — borderline high, partially offset by potassium 254 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt during preparation, as sodium accumulates with larger servings; the potassium content (254 mg/100g) provides partial offsetting benefit but does not fully neutralise the sodium load at typical serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.3 g/100g) and low sugar (1.6 g/100g); high protein moderates postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Madras is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content (6.3 g/100g) and low GI (~35); however, it should not be paired with large portions of white rice or naan, which would substantially raise the glycaemic load of the overall meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated ~2.8 g/100g from red meat (lamb); sodium 308 mg/100g; cholesterol 29 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lamb Madras in moderation (≤150 g per occasion) given its saturated fat content from red lamb meat and borderline sodium level; choosing leaner cuts and requesting reduced oil during preparation can lower the saturated fat contribution.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; tomatoes and chilli are nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb Madras is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory properties of red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6) and the presence of nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, chilli), which may exacerbate joint inflammation; substituting lamb with chicken or oily fish would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Madras is a South Indian-style curry dish popular in Singapore and across the Indian diaspora. It consists of boneless pieces of lamb slow-cooked with fresh tomatoes, onions, bell pepper, and a robust blend of South Indian spices including chilli, coriander, cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish originates from the Madras (Chennai) culinary tradition of Tamil Nadu and is characterised by its deep reddish-brown colour, bold heat, and complex spice layering. The texture is tender and saucy, with the lamb absorbing the aromatic spice base during braising. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 117 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~2.8 g), and 6.3 g carbohydrate with only 1.6 g sugar, making it a relatively low-carbohydrate, protein-rich dish. Sodium is 308 mg/100g, which is borderline high. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when served without high-GI accompaniments. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions but should be consumed cautiously by those with hypertension (due to sodium) and cardiovascular disease (due to saturated fat from red meat). It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of lamb and the presence of nightshade vegetables. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as part of a rice or bread meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb madras?
Is lamb madras suitable for diabetics?
Is lamb madras high in sodium?
What spices are in lamb madras?
Can I eat lamb madras with hypertension?
Is lamb madras good for weight loss?
What is the difference between lamb madras and lamb curry?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010855, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data as HPB does not report this field. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement for Lamb Madras exists.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Madras (G00010855) and are not available in ICMR-NIN or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290), proportioned to the HPB-reported total fat of 6.39 g/100g; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed meat curry with vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Madras has been published. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (lamb, vegetables, spices) but is preparation-dependent; kosher status is not applicable. Dietary flag is_gluten_free is set to true based on typical ingredients; cross-contamination risk exists in shared kitchen environments.
